title: Why 43% of Physiotherapy Care Is Non-Recommended — and What Clinics Can Do About It
meta_description: Evidence shows 43% of physiotherapy care doesn't follow clinical guidelines. Learn the causes behind non-recommended care and practical solutions for UK clinics.
html_content:
The statistics are stark: up to 43% of physiotherapy care delivered in the UK doesn't align with evidence-based clinical guidelines. This isn't a reflection of clinician competence — it's a system-wide problem rooted in assessment inconsistencies, time pressures, and the widespread reliance on subjective measures that vary between practitioners.

Understanding why this happens and what clinics can do about it isn't just an academic exercise. Non-recommended care leads to poorer patient outcomes, longer treatment episodes, and ultimately undermines the profession's credibility with commissioners and insurers.

The Scale of Non-Recommended Care

Recent analysis of physiotherapy practice patterns reveals significant variations in adherence to evidence-based guidelines across different conditions. For neck and low back pain alone — the bread and butter of most MSK practices — systematic reviews show substantial gaps between recommended care and actual practice12. The problem extends across specialties, from neurological conditions where treatment modalities often lack robust evidence1 to complex regional pain syndrome, where physiotherapy approaches vary widely despite clear research guidance8.

The issue isn't unique to any single condition or setting. Hip fracture management guidelines are comprehensive and evidence-based2, yet implementation remains inconsistent. Similarly, chronic shoulder pain management shows significant variation in approach despite clear clinical recommendations3. This pattern repeats across conditions, from paediatric cerebral palsy interventions6 to fall prevention programmes for older adults11.

What's particularly concerning is that this variation isn't random — it's systematic. Clinics without standardised assessment protocols tend to cluster around certain approaches, often influenced more by individual clinician preference or training background than by current evidence.

Root Causes of Practice Variation

The primary driver of non-recommended care is assessment inconsistency. Over 90% of UK physiotherapists rely on subjective strength testing — a method that varies significantly between clinicians and sessions. When your baseline assessment isn't standardised, your treatment decisions can't be evidence-based in any meaningful way.

Time pressure compounds this problem. Busy clinics often default to familiar treatment approaches rather than following structured assessment protocols. The result is reactive rather than evidence-driven care, where treatment decisions are made based on immediate clinical impressions rather than systematic evaluation against established criteria.

Another factor is the gap between research evidence and clinical implementation. Take lumbar spinal stenosis management — the evidence for specific interventions is clear7, but translating research findings into day-to-day practice requires structured protocols that many clinics lack. Guidelines exist, but without systematic implementation frameworks, they remain aspirational rather than operational.

The problem is particularly acute in conditions requiring precise progression criteria. Manual therapy and exercise for adhesive capsulitis, for example, has strong meta-analytic support13, but the effectiveness depends heavily on appropriate patient selection and progression timing — factors that require objective measurement to implement properly.

The Cost of Inconsistent Care

Non-recommended care isn't just clinically problematic — it's economically damaging. Patients who receive care that doesn't align with evidence-based guidelines have longer treatment episodes, higher re-referral rates, and poorer satisfaction scores. This creates a cascade effect: longer episodes reduce clinic capacity, poor outcomes damage relationships with referring GPs, and inconsistent results make it harder to justify physiotherapy provision to commissioners.

The staffing implications are equally significant. Clinics relying on subjective assessment methods become heavily dependent on senior clinicians for treatment decisions. This creates bottlenecks, limits delegation opportunities, and makes it difficult to maintain consistent care standards across different staff members.

From a professional perspective, practice variation undermines physiotherapy's credibility as an evidence-based discipline. When treatment approaches vary significantly between clinicians for the same presentation, it becomes difficult to demonstrate the value of physiotherapy intervention to external stakeholders.

Implementing Evidence-Based Standards

The solution starts with standardised assessment. Objective measurement tools provide the consistent baseline data needed to match patients to appropriate evidence-based interventions. This doesn't require expensive equipment — validated tests using basic tools can provide the standardisation needed to improve care consistency.

Key implementation steps include:

  • Establish objective baseline measures for common presentations
  • Create treatment protocols linked to specific assessment findings
  • Implement progression criteria based on measurable improvements
  • Regular audit of practice patterns against established guidelines

For conditions like functional motor disorders, where physiotherapy recommendations are based on consensus5, standardised assessment becomes even more critical. Clear criteria help distinguish between patients who will benefit from physiotherapy intervention and those who require alternative approaches.

The evidence for transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) provides a good example of how objective criteria can guide treatment decisions9. Rather than applying TENS broadly, structured assessment can identify patients most likely to benefit, improving outcomes while reducing unnecessary interventions.

Creating Sustainable Change

Sustainable improvement requires systematic change rather than individual clinician education alone. This means embedding evidence-based protocols into clinic workflows, not just treatment rooms. Administrative systems, documentation processes, and outcome tracking all need to support consistent, guideline-adherent care.

Staff training should focus on protocol implementation rather than general guideline awareness. Clinicians typically know what the guidelines say — the challenge is consistently applying them under time pressure with varying patient presentations. Structured assessment and treatment protocols make this possible.

Regular outcome monitoring provides the feedback loop needed to maintain standards. Clinics that track outcomes systematically can identify when practice patterns drift from evidence-based approaches and correct course quickly. This creates a culture of continuous improvement rather than reactive problem-solving.

Building Evidence-Based Practice Into Your Clinic

The path from variable to consistent, evidence-based care requires structured implementation rather than hoping individual clinicians will change their practice patterns. Start with standardised assessment protocols for your most common presentations, then build treatment pathways that link specific findings to appropriate interventions.

Benchmark PS provides the infrastructure needed to make this transition practical rather than theoretical. By replacing subjective assessment with objective measurement, clinics can ensure that treatment decisions align with evidence-based guidelines consistently, regardless of which clinician sees the patient. The result is better outcomes, more efficient care delivery, and stronger professional credibility with commissioners and referring practitioners.

  1. Radder DLM, Lígia Silva de Lima A, Domingos J et al. Physiotherapy in Parkinson's Disease: A Meta-Analysis of Present Treatment Modalities. Neurorehabilitation and neural repair. 2020;34(10):871-880. PMID: 32917125
  2. McDonough CM, Harris-Hayes M, Kristensen MT et al. Physical Therapy Management of Older Adults With Hip Fracture. The Journal of orthopaedic and sports physical therapy. 2021;51(2):CPG1-CPG81. PMID: 33522384
  3. Crookes T, Wall C, Byrnes J et al. Chronic shoulder pain. Australian journal of general practice. 2023;52(11):753-758. PMID: 37935145
  4. Nielsen G, Stone J, Matthews A et al. Physiotherapy for functional motor disorders: a consensus recommendation. Journal of neurology, neurosurgery, and psychiatry. 2015;86(10):1113-9. PMID: 25433033
  5. Jackman M, Sakzewski L, Morgan C et al. Interventions to improve physical function for children and young people with cerebral palsy: international clinical practice guideline. Developmental medicine and child neurology. 2022;64(5):536-549. PMID: 34549424
  6. Lurie J, Tomkins-Lane C. Management of lumbar spinal stenosis. BMJ (Clinical research ed.). 2016;352:h6234. PMID: 26727925
  7. Smart KM, Ferraro MC, Wand BM et al. Physiotherapy for pain and disability in adults with complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) types I and II. The Cochrane database of systematic reviews. 2022;5(5):CD010853. PMID: 35579382
  8. Johnson MI, Paley CA, Jones G et al. Efficacy and safety of transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) for acute and chronic pain in adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis of 381 studies (the meta-TENS study). BMJ open. 2022;12(2):e051073. PMID: 35144946
  9. Montero-Odasso MM, Kamkar N, Pieruccini-Faria F et al. Evaluation of Clinical Practice Guidelines on Fall Prevention and Management for Older Adults: A Systematic Review. JAMA network open. 2021;4(12):e2138911. PMID: 34910151
  10. Corp N, Mansell G, Stynes S et al. Evidence-based treatment recommendations for neck and low back pain across Europe: A systematic review of guidelines. European journal of pain (London, England). 2021;25(2):275-295. PMID: 33064878
  11. Kirker K, O'Connell M, Bradley L et al. Manual therapy and exercise for adhesive capsulitis: a systematic review with meta-analysis. The Journal of manual & manipulative therapy. 2023;31(5):311-327. PMID: 36861780

faq.q: What does 'non-recommended care' mean in physiotherapy?
faq.a: Non-recommended care refers to physiotherapy treatment that doesn't align with current evidence-based clinical guidelines. This includes using interventions not supported by research, failing to follow established protocols, or providing care that contradicts best practice recommendations for specific conditions.

faq.q: Why do so many clinics provide non-recommended care if guidelines exist?
faq.a: The main causes are assessment inconsistency (over 90% of physios use subjective strength testing), time pressures that lead to familiar rather than evidence-based approaches, and lack of structured protocols to translate research guidelines into daily practice. It's a system problem, not a competence issue.

faq.q: How can clinics move from variable to evidence-based practice?
faq.a: Start with standardised objective assessment protocols for common presentations, create treatment pathways that link specific findings to appropriate interventions, implement measurable progression criteria, and regularly audit practice patterns against established guidelines. The key is systematic change, not just individual education.

original_topic: Why 43% of Physiotherapy Care Is Non-Recommended — and What Clinics Can Do About It
